records.ety: [OE. fangen, fongen, fon (g orig. only in p. p. and imp. tense), AS. fōn; akin to D. vangen, OHG. fāhan, G. fahen, fangen, Icel. fā, Sw. f, fnga, Dan. fange, faae, Goth. fahan, and prob. to E. fair, peace, pact. Cf. Fair, a.]

defs: To catch; to seize, as with the teeth; to lay hold of; to gripe; to clutch.
quotes: He's in the law's clutches; you see he's fanged.

defs: To enable to catch or tear; to furnish with fangs.

records.ety: [From Fang, v. t.; cf. AS. fang a taking, booty, G. fang.]

defs: The tusk of an animal, by which the prey is seized and held or torn; a long pointed tooth; esp., one of the usually erectile, venomous teeth of serpents. Also, one of the falcers of a spider.
quotes: Since I am a dog, beware my fangs.

defs: Any shoot or other thing by which hold is taken.
quotes: The protuberant fangs of the yucca.

defs: The root, or one of the branches of the root, of a tooth. See Tooth.
A niche in the side of an adit or shaft, for an air course.
A projecting tooth or prong, as in a part of a lock, or the plate of a belt clamp, or the end of a tool, as a chisel, where it enters the handle.
The valve of a pump box.
A bend or loop of a rope.
